QF.id: Clubbing, Set-Off and Carry Forward of Losses_22

question.text: Mr. Prakash transferred property worth Rs.20,00,000 to his HUF (Hindu Undivided Family). The property generates rental income of Rs.1,80,000 per year. Under which section and in whose hands is this income taxable?

answer.text: HUF's income under Section 22, as the property now belongs to the HUF
Clubbed with Prakash's income under Section 64(2), as he transferred the asset to HUF without adequate consideration
Split between Prakash and HUF in proportion to their respective shares
Exempt from tax as transfers to HUF are not treated as transfers

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under Section 64(2), when an individual transfers an asset to the HUF without adequate consideration, the income from such asset is clubbed with the individual who made the transfer. The rental income of Rs.1,80,000 will therefore be included in Prakash's total income, not the HUF's. **The Trap:** Option A is the most common error — students assume that once property belongs to HUF, income is HUF's. But Section 64(2) specifically catches this tax planning strategy. **Cross-Exam Context:** HUF-related clubbing under Section 64(2) is unique to NISM X-B; X-A typically does not cover HUF taxation in depth. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** HUF is a powerful tax planning vehicle, but throwing personal assets into HUF doesn't help — the income bounces back to the transferor. Only HUF's own earnings and ancestral property income remain with HUF.
